summary: DHH argues against the lawsuit investigation targeting GitHub's Copilot AI, framing it as scarcity thinking that betrays the spirit of open source. He dismisses claims that Copilot will create a walled garden that starves open source communities, calling it classic zero-sum thinking. He views Copilot as exactly the kind of remix and reuse that open source was meant to enable. He draws a parallel to Oracle's attempt to copyright Java APIs, warning that the open source community risks becoming the gatekeepers it was meant to fight.
key_points: The lawsuit investigation against Copilot employs scarcity scarecrow tactics that contradict the collaborative spirit of open source
The claim that AI code completion will destroy open source communities is illogical because half the value of open source is ongoing community collaboration, not one-off code snippets
If we indict AI intermediation of open source code, the same logic would condemn CDNs, package bundlers, and other tools that serve code without directing users to project homepages
The investigation mirrors Oracle's attempt to copyright Java APIs—using law to lock software into scarcity thinking
Open source creators who share code freely should welcome innovations like Copilot that build upon their work in creative ways
money_quotes: I love the ethos of open source: Free code creating a true commons in software.
This is classic zero-sum thinking. The kind that open source at its best transcends for me, and Open Source at its worst entrenches.
Isn't this partly why we share our code to begin with? To enable others to remix, reuse, and regenerate with?

There's a funny mirror version of this investigation that looks an awfully lot like Oracle's tragic attempt to copyright the Java APIs. To use the law to lock critical software into this dungeon of scarcity thinking.
Aren't these the demons we in the open source community were meant to be fighting rather than become?
These are the ways of closed software. The greed with which free use is often curtailed, unless it's explicitly allowed.
To escape this trap of hoarding positive rights, lest someone, somewhere, might get away with some inspired feat of unauthorized creativity that builds upon my work.
key_insight: The open source community risks betraying its own values by applying scarcity thinking to AI tools like Copilot that remix and build upon freely shared code—the very thing open source was designed to enable.
